coredata.dc:title: Preparation of antimicrobial/ultraviolet protective bacterial nanocellulose film with carbon dots synthesized from lactic acid bacteria
coredata.dc:creator: Kousheh, Seyedeh Alaleh
Moradi, Mehran
Tajik, Hossein
Molaei, Rahim
coredata.dc:description: Microbial biomass, as an environmentally friendly resource, has drawn a lot of attention as green biomaterials for production of unique and functionalized carbon dots (CDs). However, the hydrophilicity and high porosity drive bacterial nanocellulose as a suitable matrix for CDs with antimicrobial, photoluminescent and ultraviolet blocking features. For the first time, antimicrobial CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method from cell-free supernatant of Lactobacillus acidophilus and characterized. Antimicrobial performance of CDs was examined on Escherichia coli (Gram-negative) and Listeria monocytogenes (Gram-positive). Additionally, the as-prepared CDs embedded by ex-situ method into nanocellulose in order to fabricate antimicrobial/ultraviolet protective nanopaper. The photoluminescent CDs with an average size of 2.8 nm and high-hydroxylated groups were synthesized. The CDs at 500 mg mL−1 concentration had antibacterial activity towards both bacteria. Moreover, nanopaper displayed a fluorescence appearance under ultraviolet. Nanocellulose with CDs loading capacity of 71.74 ± 4.13 mg cm−2 represented an appropriate stretchability and flexibility in comparison to nanocellulose. The CDs incorporated nanopaper also depicted greater ultraviolet-blocking specifications and inhibitory activity on Gram-positive bacterium than Gram-negative one. CDs can be used as a novel fluorescence antimicrobial/ultraviolet protective material in the nanocellulose film in order to develop an antimicrobial and forgery-proof packaging due to their fluorescence appearance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Active packaging
Antimicrobial film
Lactobacillus
Probiotic
Antioxidant
UV protective

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Size, morphology and spectral attributes of CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic of CDs preparation via the hydrothermal method.

The intensity distribution histogram shows narrow size distribution and >98% of CQDs were in size < 10 nm with an average size of 2.8 nm. The TEM image of synthesized CDs shows highly homogeneous CDs with <10 nm in size.

FTIR spectra of bacterial nanocellulose (BNC; A), the synthesized CDs (B) and CD-BNC (C). The elimination of OH bending vibration (1302 cm−1, B) in the BNC spectrum affirms the interaction between CDs and BNC through the formation of hydrogen bonds.
A sharp peak at 365 nm was recorded in CDs. The CDs solution represented dark brown and green glow under white light and UV lamp, respectively.

No significant reduction in the antimicrobial activity of CDs (represented as the zone of inhibition; mm) was observed in aqueous solvent against L. monocytogenes and E. coli during 8 weeks storage at refrigerator (4 °C) and room (22 °C) temperatures.

DPPH radical scavenging of CDs (A). The CDs solution with DPPH (left to right; 5, 1, 0.1, 0.05 and 0.005 mg mL−1) before (B) and after (C) incubation in dark-ambient temperature represented gradual bleaching of DPPH solution by the CDs (RS: radical scavenging).

The ultimate tensile strength of BNC has decreased after impregnation in CDs solution with a loading capacity of 71.8 mg cm−2. A significant increment in the strain to break was reported by increasing the CDs loading capacity of BNC.

The FE-SEM micrographs confirmed high porous network with a homogeneous distribution of BNC fibers (A) and illustrate its entangled fibrous and high porous network with homogeneous distribution. A compact structure was revealed in CD-BNC nanopaper (B) (mag. 2000K×).

The photographs of the BNC and BNC membrane with different CDs loading capacities solutions under white light and UV lamp (A). BNC with higher loading capacity represented intense green-light-emitting appearance with no apparent CDs clusters. The transmission spectra of BNC and CD-BNC nanopapers in the range of 200–800 nm (B); a transmittance < 1% at <548 nm was rescored in BNC with CDs loading capacity of 15.11 ± 2.47 mg cm−2.

Zone of inhibition (mm) of pure BNC and BNC loaded with CDs against L. monocytogenes (A) and E. coli (B). The BNC and BNC with CDs loading capacity of 27.19 ± 3.55, 51.47 ± 2.18 mg cm−2 did not show antimicrobial activity.
Antimicrobial activity (diameter zones of inhibition; mm) of CDs and CDs incorporated BNC membrane.

row.entry: CDs loading capacity of BNC (mg cm−2)

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Different superscripts letters in each column mean that the values are significantly different (p ≤ 0.05).

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: According to the well diffusion method.
According to the disc covering method.

head.ce:title: Preparation of antimicrobial/ultraviolet protective bacterial nanocellulose film with carbon dots synthesized from lactic acid bacteria

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food Hygiene and Quality Control, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Urmia University, Urmia, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Food Hygiene and Quality Control

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food Hygiene and Quality Control, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Urmia University, Urmia, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Microbial biomass, as an environmentally friendly resource, has drawn a lot of attention as green biomaterials for production of unique and functionalized carbon dots (CDs). However, the hydrophilicity and high porosity drive bacterial nanocellulose as a suitable matrix for CDs with antimicrobial, photoluminescent and ultraviolet blocking features. For the first time, antimicrobial CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method from cell-free supernatant of Lactobacillus acidophilus and characterized. Antimicrobial performance of CDs was examined on Escherichia coli (Gram-negative) and Listeria monocytogenes (Gram-positive). Additionally, the as-prepared CDs embedded by ex-situ method into nanocellulose in order to fabricate antimicrobial/ultraviolet protective nanopaper. The photoluminescent CDs with an average size of 2.8 nm and high-hydroxylated groups were synthesized. The CDs at 500 mg mL−1 concentration had antibacterial activity towards both bacteria. Moreover, nanopaper displayed a fluorescence appearance under ultraviolet. Nanocellulose with CDs loading capacity of 71.74 ± 4.13 mg cm−2 represented an appropriate stretchability and flexibility in comparison to nanocellulose. The CDs incorporated nanopaper also depicted greater ultraviolet-blocking specifications and inhibitory activity on Gram-positive bacterium than Gram-negative one. CDs can be used as a novel fluorescence antimicrobial/ultraviolet protective material in the nanocellulose film in order to develop an antimicrobial and forgery-proof packaging due to their fluorescence appearance.

ce:section.ce:para: The unique characteristics of nano-based materials have led to their massive application in various fields of the food industry including detection and identification of contaminants, food safety and packaging, membrane filtration and food additives [1,2]. In food packaging, the nanomaterials can be applied as 1) nanosensor for shelf life and quality monitoring and pathogens identification and 2) nanostructures to improve mechanical, structural, physical [3], and barrier attributes of packaging materials [4] and additionally for the fabrication of antimicrobial films [5]. Generally, the immobilization of antimicrobial nanostructures in food packaging elements and manufacturing of nanocomposite and nanolaminates can extend the shelf life and assure the safety of food commodities [4]. On the other hand, providing a material that fulfills both of these goals is extremely valuable and economical for the food packaging industry.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a class of quantum dots with unique attributes such as photostability, high sustainability, extraordinary optical, antibacterial and antioxidant features with low cost, simple mass production, and low toxicity [6–8]. They do not exhibit apparent toxicity [9,10]. These characteristics render CDs very favorable for application in the broad fields including bio-imaging, biosensing, ultraviolet (UV)-barrier and packaging [11]. Also, the ultra-small size and surface functional groups of CDs resulted in their inhibitory effect against bacteria cells [8]. Previously, the antimicrobial potential of CDs on different types of bacterial species including gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria has been demonstrated [8,12–14].

Bacterial nanocellulose (BNC) is a naturally occurring nanostructure biopolymer with a similar structure and molecular formula to that of plants. The BNC produced through a bottom-up process by Komagataeibacter genus and certain acetic acid bacteria and composes ribbon-shaped fibrils with 70–80 nm in width and a 3D nanofibrillar arrangement [15,16]. Different types of guest bio-compounds can be incorporated into BNC membrane in two different ways including impregnation (ex-situ) and bottom-up built methods to develop novel bionanocomposites for active and smart food packaging. Actually, the water holding capacity of BNC in the undried state is usually 60–700 times higher than the dry form [15].

The UV barrier potential of nano-based materials, as well as other natural and plant-based substances in polymeric substrates, has attracted a lot of attention [17,18]. Recently, UV-photo reduction activity of nanomaterials including silver nanoparticles (NP) [19] and TiO2 NPs [17] in polymeric films has been broadly investigated. Besides, superior UV-blocking in chitosan–CD films [20] and in regenerated cellulose-based film [10] was also reported.

Many approaches have been proposed for the synthesis of CDs including chemical/electrochemical oxidation, microwave irradiation, laser ablation, ultrasonic technique, and hydrothermal/solvothermal syntheses [21]. In particular, the hydrothermal method is one of the most commonly used procedures in CDs synthesis. This green synthesis refers to the synthesis of CDs from smaller carbon units (small organic molecules) through a four-stage procedure (condensation, polymerization, carbonization, and passivation) [22]. Consequently, the bio-extracted, organic molecules and/or polymers (in aqueous or organic media) merge together at relatively high temperatures to form carbon seeding cores and then grow into CDs with the desired particle size.

Actually, the hydrothermal method is a safe, efficient and inexpensive method for CDs synthesis. In this context, a wide range of precursors such as organic chemicals, biomass, biodegradable waste, and by-products have applied for fabrication of CDs by a hydrothermal carbonization process. Generally, the CDs can be derived from chemical precursors and food [23,24] by sophisticated techniques [6]. Recently, biodegradable waste and by-products [7] and microbial biomass [25] have drawn a lot of attention as non-toxic and environmentally-friendly materials in green methods of synthesis. Although some works have successfully conducted on the use of some bacterial strains, Staphylococcus aureus, Escherichia coli [12] and Bacillus cereus [26], for the synthesis of CDs, the utilization of value-added microbial by-products has not been investigated. A creative approach is the use of cell-free supernatant (CFS) of probiotics. CFS is a value-added unused product of large-scale fermentation process of beneficial bacteria (e.g., probiotics). Although, CFS of Lactobacillus spp. has specific functional effects (e.g., antimicrobial antioxidant and antibiofilm activity) [27,28], it can be utilized as a sole carbon source for CDs due to the presence of organic compounds such as succinamic acid, cyclopentane, 1-cyclododecanone, undecanoic acid, cycloheptane, phenol, and benzoic acid [27].

It is expected that the addition of water-soluble and photoluminescent biomaterials such as CDs from probiotic bacteria increases the antimicrobial/antioxidant and UV protective attributes of the polymeric system, which improves their potential application as a novel biomaterial for antimicrobial and UV-protective food active packaging. In this study, for the first time, CDs were synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal method from CFS of L. acidophilus and extensively characterized. The as-synthesized CDs were introduced into the BNC membrane, produced from Komagataeibacter xylinus, in order to fabricate a functionalized nanopaper with antimicrobial and improved UV-light barrier attributes.

The E. coli ATCC 25922, Listeria monocytogenes ATCC 19115, K. xylinus subsp. sucrofermentants BPR 2001 and L. acidophilus LA-5® were obtained from the culture collection of Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Urmia University. Gluten and molasses were kindly acquired from Faravari fructose NAB company (Tehran, Iran) and Miandoab Sugar Factory (Miandoab, Iran), respectively. The modified Man, Rogosa and Sharpe (m-MRS) and Tryptic Soy (TS) broth media were purchased from Quelab Laboratories (Quebec, Canada) and agar-agar powder from Mirmedia (Tehran, Iran). Sterile PTFE syringe filters were purchased from Millipore Inc. (MA, USA), ethanol from Pars Alcohol (Shiraz, Iran), and DPPH (2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl-hydrate) from Sigma-Aldrich, (MO, USA) and other materials were supplied by Merck (Darmstadt, Germany).

CDs were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method [29] from CFS of L. acidophilus. The schematic of CDs preparation is shown in Fig. 1 . Prior to the experiment, the probiotic bacterium was sub-cultured in m-MRS broth and incubated at 37 ± 1 °C for 48 h under anaerobic conditions. Next, the bacterial suspension was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 15 min and the CFS was carefully collected and transferred to a stainless-steel chamber. Following 24 h incubation at 200 °C, the dark brown solution was obtained and filtered by a sterile syringe filter (0.42 μm), to eliminate large particles. Finally, CDs were lyophilized (freezing temperature of −40 °C, pump pressure of 100 mTorr and shelf temperature of −60 °C) (Zist Farayand Tajhiz Sahand, Tabriz, Iran) and stored in aseptic condition at 4 °C for further experiments. The m-MRS media itself (without the bacterium) was also used for the synthesis of CDs (m-CDs) as a control with a similar procedure as mentioned above.

Prior to measuring UV–visible absorption and transmittance, CDs aqueous solution was centrifuged at 13,000 rpm for 15 min and then examined via a double beam spectrophotometer (T80+ UV–Vis spectrophotometer PG, Beijing, China) with a resolution of 1 nm at 190 to 800 nm. Identification of structural properties and surface groups of lyophilized CDs was performed by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Thermo Nicolet instrument, Nexus® 670, USA) at wavelength of 4000–450 cm−1. In order to investigate the size and morphology of as-synthesized CDs, particle size analyzer (PSA) (Scatteroscope, Qudix Inc., South Korea) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Leo 906 E, Carl Zeiss, Germany) were used. Laser diffraction particle analyzer (Nanotrac wave II; Microtrac, Krefeld, Germany) was employed to calculate zeta potential of CDs aqueous solution at 23 °C.

Based on the importance of E. coli (gram-negative) and L. monocytogenes (gram-positive) as foodborne pathogens, they were selected for antimicrobial study. Both bacteria were sub-cultured in TS broth from frozen stocks (at −20 °C) and incubated at 37 ± 1 °C for 18 h. The optical density (OD600 nm) of suspensions was adjusted to ~0.1, representing ~8 log10 CFU mL−1. The bacterial counts were confirmed by plating on TS agar after 24 h incubation at 37 ± 1 °C. Antibacterial potential of 500, 250 and 100 mg mL−1 of CDs were determined by agar well diffusion method. Initially, an as-prepared inoculum of bacteria (~6 log10 CFU mL−1) was spread on the surface of TS agar. Then, under aseptic conditions, wells (8 mm in diameter) were punched using a cork borer into an agar, filled with 100 μL of CDs, and after 24 h incubation (at 37 ± 1 °C), the diameter zones of inhibition were carefully measured [30]. The as-prepared CDs were maintained at room (22 ± 1 °C) and refrigerated (4 ± 1 °C) temperatures and the stability of antimicrobial activity during storage for 8 weeks was also investigated according to the method mentioned above.

The radical scavenging activity of CDs was evaluated in vitro using methanolic DPPH solution (4 mg 100 mL−1 methanol) [31]. For this purpose, known volumes of DPPH solution were added to a series of aqueous solutions of lyophilized CDs with different concentrations, incubated at room temperature for 1 h in dark condition, and the absorption of each sample was recorded at 517 nm. Finally, the percentage of radical scavenging (% RS) was estimated according to the following equation (Eq. (1)):
(1)
% RS =
Ac − As
Ac
× 100
Ac and As indicate the absorption of control (DPPH solution) and CDs solution containing DPPH, respectively. Butylated hydroxyl toluene (BHT) was used as a control.

The BNC membrane was synthesized under static condition according to the method of Rodrigues et al. [32]. The culture medium, containing disodium hydrogen phosphate 0.27% (w/v), citric acid 0.115% (w/v), gluten 1.91% (w/v), molasses 5.38% (w/v), ethanol 1.38% (v/v), and ammonium sulphate 0.63% (w/v), was prepared and sterilized. The K. xylinus inoculum was initially prepared as described previously [32]. The inoculated medium was incubated under the static condition for 3 days and the synthesized BNC membrane was removed, washed and decolorized. The purified BNC was maintained at 4 °C until use.

The CD-BNC nanopapers were fabricated by impregnation of previously sterilized (UV light; 2 min) pieces of BNC in defined weight into each CDs concentration (1, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 250, and 500 mg mL−1) for 24 h at room temperature. The extra free solution on CDs loaded BNC was removed with a filter paper and the BNC was weighed. The CDs loading capacity of BNC was explored spectrophotometrically by measuring the absorbance value at 365 nm and comparing the difference between the amount of CDs before and after impregnation. The results were expressed as the amount of absorbed CDs per cm2 of dry BNC [33].

The strain to break (STB) (%) and the ultimate tensile strength (UTS) (MPa) of preconditioned (by MgNO3 solution (53 ± 2% RH) at 25 ± 1 °C for 48 h in a desiccator) CD-BNC nanopaper and pure BNC membrane were examined by a universal testing instrument STM20 (Santam, Iran) [34]. The effect of CDs on morphology, homogeneity and the porosity structure of the BNC membrane was examined using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) (SIGMA VP-500, Zeiss, Germany). The UV–visible transmittance and interaction of functional groups of CDs with BNC were also examined as mentioned earlier.

Disc covering antimicrobial assay was applied to investigate the antimicrobial activity of CD-BNC nanopaper [30]. Similar to agar well diffusion, the TS agar was inoculated with bacterial suspension and then, circular discs of CD-BNC nanopaper and BNC membrane were aseptically placed on the agar. The sensitivity of bacteria was determined by measuring diameter zones of inhibition after 24 h incubation at 37 ± 1 °C. The CFS and m-CDs were applied as negative controls.

All experiments were performed at least in three replicates and data were analyzed by analysis of variance (ANOVA) in the software of Graph Pad Prism, version 8.01 (GraphPad Software, USA). Mean differences were compared by Duncan's multiple range test at 95% confidence level.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Size, morphology and spectral attributes of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Ideally, the synthesis of value-added CDs from green resources occurs in a fast, efficient, economical way with high yield and without applying higher temperatures [35]. In this respect, bacterial biomass is an interesting alternative as a carbon source for the fabrication of CDs due to their abundance and facile access throughout the world. In this study, CFS of probiotic was applied, for the first time, for fabrication CDs. A series of experiments was conducted in order to characterize the synthesized CDs. The average dimension and size distribution of as-synthesized CDs were firstly characterized by PSA. As shown in Fig. 2 , CDs had narrow size distribution and >98% of CQDs were in size < 10 nm with an average size of 2.8 nm. This was further confirmed by TEM analysis. The captured TEM micrograph (Fig. 2) represents highly homogenous CDs without agglomeration and the size of <10 nm. CDs with an average size distribution of 3.1 nm and spherical in shape were fabricated from lemon juice by hydrothermal reaction [23].

The nature of surface groups of synthesized CDs was studied using the FTIR. The obtained spectra at the wavenumber range of 450–4000 cm−1 at a resolution of 1 cm−1 were compared in Fig. 3 . In the case of the CDs (Fig. 3B), the broad bands between 3400 and 3200 cm−1, and sharp bond at 1668 cm−1 can be related to the stretching vibration and in-plane bending vibration of NH/OH and CO stretching vibration, respectively. As well, the other characteristic peaks are 1595, 1450, 1302 and 1118 cm−1 corresponding to CO stretching, COOH bending, OH bending and CO stretching vibration which can certify the presence of organic acids on CDs surfaces [36,37].

The optical and visual properties of water-soluble CDs were also evaluated by recording the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the prepared CDs with a resolution of 5 nm between 250 and 800 nm and visually monitoring of solutions color under white light and UV lamp. As shown in Fig. 4 , the surface plasmon resonance band of CDs exhibits a sharp peak at 365 nm that is evidently corresponding to the successfully synthesized CDs through carbonization of CFS. As illustrated in Fig. 4, the CFS (control) solution is yellow under white light, while under UV excitation, no green glow was observed in the solution. The color of the CDs changed from dark brown to intense green glow under UV lamp due to quantum confinement effect. These findings on florescence glow of CDs are consistent with other studies [8,29]. In a similar study, CDs with strong green fluorescence under UV light and absorption peaks at 270 to 380 nm was synthesized from Lawsonia inermis (Henna) plant according to the hydrothermal method [8]. The quantum size of CDs and various surface trap sites are exactly related to the typical fluorescence mechanism of CDs [38].

It can be seen from Table 1 that CDs at only 500 mg mL−1 have antibacterial activity towards both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. A concentration and contact time-dependent antibacterial performance of CDs on different bacterial species were previously reported [39]. Comparable values were reported in case of CDs derived from tamarind on S. aureus, Klebsiella pneumoniae and Pseudomonas aeruginosa [40]. However, they exhibited potent inhibitory activity on the Gram-positive bacterium than Gram-negative one. The bacterial cell wall is a decisive factor in bacterial defense against antimicrobial agents. Despite thick cell wall in Gram-positive bacteria, Gram-negative bacteria possess a thin but more complex wall which provides various functional groups on the surface of bacteria [8]. A similar results reporting by Shahshahanipour et al. [8] and Al Awak et al. [13], using E. coli as a Gram-negative bacterium and S. aureus and Bacillus subtilis as Gram-positive bacteria, respectively. Owing to ultra-small size and high surface/volume ratio, CDs affect the bacteria via different routes, including free radical production and oxidizing [8], and membrane disruption through covering the surface of cell and disrupting it [14]. The CFSs of probiotic bacteria contain divers organic molecules such as mono- and disaccharides, organic acids, and phenolic compounds [27]. Those substances are perfectly adapted for the synthesis of CDs since they can be rapidly polymerized and carbonized and create a carbon backbone by the heat arising from a specific exothermic reaction rather than heating equipment [21,22]. Accordingly, CFS can be suitable as a precursor for CD synthesis. On the other hand, the possibility of producing CDs from m-MRS culture medium in a similar way to CFS was also investigated. The synthesized m-MRS solution had neither fluorescence and nor antimicrobial activities, owning to the poor chemical composition for the hydrothermal fabrication of CD in comparison to CFS.

Generally, the surface charge of bacteria and antibacterial agents are effective on susceptibility of microorganisms to antimicrobial agents. The zeta potential of CDs in the aqueous solution was −24.5 mV, confirmed the presence of negative groups (carboxyl, hydroxyl, and carbonyl) on the surface of the CDs. While the zeta potential of L. monocytogenes and E. coli are −2.5 mV [41] and − 4.95 mV [42], respectively, showing the ability of CDs to attach and penetrate into L. monocytogenes in an easier way than E. coli, because of lower negative surface charges of L. monocytogenes. The stability of the antibacterial activity of antimicrobial compounds during storage is an essential attribute for commercial usage. According to Fig. 5 , no significant reduction in the antimicrobial activity of CDs in aqueous solvent has been observed over two months storage at refrigerator (4 °C) and room (22 °C) temperatures. This durability of CDs over a period of time was due to the presence of negative groups on the surface of CDs and its zeta potential (−24.5 mV).

The scavenging activity of nanostructures, i.e. CDs, is mainly related to the number of electron donating groups found on the surface of the material. The radical scavenging potential of CDs was determined through the DPPH test by absorption measurement at 517 nm (Fig. 6A). The DPPH solution was deep violet and after adding to different concentrations of CDs (Fig. 6B), the color of solutions became lighter within an hour (Fig. 6C). It is well known that fading may be due to the presence of numerous electron-donating groups like hydroxyl and carboxyl on the CDs surface [43].

The IC50 is used to show the antioxidant potential of a substance and defines as the concentration of an antioxidant agent needed to scavenge 50% of the initial DPPH radicals. It can be estimated from linear regression plots. According to Fig. 6A, fabricated CDs revealed scavenging activity in a dose-dependent manner. This capability was due to electron-donating groups, originating from the compounds of CFS [44]. The IC50 of CDs (1.75 μg mL−1) was less than the BHT (3.8 μg mL−1) which signifies a higher antioxidant activity of developed CDs than the CDs synthesized in previous studies [7,43,45]. A weak radical scavenging potential was reported for CDs fabricated from garlic [46].

A linear CD loading of 0.76 ± 0.11, 1.82 ± 0.63, 3.59 ± 1.03, 6.44 ± 0.94, 15.11 ± 2.47, 27.19 ± 3.55, 51.47 ± 2.18 and 71.74 ± 4.13 mg cm−2 were reported for BNC impregnated in CDs solutions of 1, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 250, and 500 mg mL−1, respectively.

The addition of nanostructures into polymeric membranes alters the mechanical features. The nature of modification appertain to the type, shape and the size of the nanostructures, the amount and method of use and the compatibility of the polymer and nanomaterial. Both investigated mechanical parameters, STB and the UTS, were extremely influenced by CDs (Fig. 7 ). The results showed that the UTS of BNC (6.75 MPa) was decreased to <0.01 MPa after impregnation in 500 mg mL−1 of CDs. The superior loading capacity of BNC, the method of impregnation [30] and heterogeneous dispersion of CDs in BNC play a principal function in this reduction. Similar reduction in UTS of BNC after addition of MgO NPs by the ex-situ method has been reported [47]. The STB was significantly (p ≤ 0.05) increased in a concentration-dependent trend from 1.03% to 67.67% in BNC impregnated in 500 mg mL−1 of CDs representing a CD-BNC nanopaper with appropriate stretchability and flexibility in comparison with pure BNC membrane due to the strong interaction between BNC and the CDs. These results are in harmony with some previous works [48,49] that showed the addition of antimicrobial compounds improved STB in different polymers. The improved STB value in CD-BNC nanopaper can be closely connected to the presence of exopolysaccharide and fatty acids in the postbiotics of lactic acid bacteria, since those materials display a plasticizing activity and strengthen the stretchability of film [50].

Due to the blackish-brown appearance, the addition of CDs into polymeric matrix changes the color of developed nanopaper. Subsequently, after incorporation of CDs into BNC, the color of BNC turned into dark brown. The obtained BNC founded more flexible with a thickness of 2.29 mm and microscopically uniform, while its macroscopic structure remained unchanged. The SEM micrographs confirm the nano dimensions of BNC and illustrate its entangled fibrous and high porous network with homogeneous distribution (Fig. 8A) suggesting the appropriate entrapping of CDs on the BNC surface due to the interaction between BNC and CDs (Fig. 8B). As depicted in Fig. 8B, CDs were well loaded on BNC with satisfying dispersion and filled the gaps. Comparable homogeneous and compact structure was reported in carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) integrated with CDs through a simple hybrid process [48].

The interaction of BNC and CDs was examined using the FTIR. As displayed in spectrum A (Fig. 3), the spectral absorption band around 3428 and 2920 cm−1 can be assigned to the OH stretching vibration and CH bond stretching (methyl and methylene groups) in BNC, respectively. Additionally, the spectral absorption band located at 1522 and 1425 cm−1 can be attributed to the bending vibrations of CH2 and CH, while the broad spectral band located between 1250 and 1000 cm−1 could be ascribed to vibration modes of CC and CO stretching and the bending mode of CH bonds of the glycosidic ether bonds and pyranoid rings of BNC structure [51,52]. In the typical spectrum of CD-BNC nanopaper (Fig. 3C), the observed shift is related to the stretching vibration of hydroxyl groups from 3428 to 3406 cm−1 (spectra A–C) and significant splitting occurred for carboxylic bending around 1400 cm−1 in spectra C compared with absorption bands around 1450 cm−1 (spectra B). The elimination of OH bending vibration (1302 cm−1, B) in the BNC membrane spectrum affirms the interaction between CDs and BNC through the formation of hydrogen bonds.

To investigate UV-light barrier properties, BNC membranes with the loading capacities of 0.76 ± 0.11, 1.82 ± 0.63, 3.59 ± 1.03, 6.44 ± 0.94, 15.11 ± 2.47, 27.19 ± 3.55, 51.47 ± 2.18 and 71.74 ± 4.13 mg cm−2 were fabricated. Fig. 9 represents photographs of the BNC and CD-BNC nanopaper under white light and UV lamp. Under UV light (Fig. 9A), no perceivable green fluorescent was seen in BNC membrane with CDs loading capacity of 6.44 ± 0.94 mg cm−2, while BNC with higher loading capacity represented intense green-light-emitting appearance. Additionally, no apparent CDs clusters were found on fluorescent nanopaper, signifying a uniform and effective loading of CDs in the nanopaper by ex-situ method. This unique specification can help to fabricate a UV protective packaging by embedding CDs into glass, paper, and polymeric food packaging materials. Furthermore, the transmission spectra of BNC membrane and CD-BNC nanopaper were recorded in the range of 200 to 800 nm (Fig. 9B). It is clear that as the amount of CDs in BNC increased, the light-blocking domain expands, then, the recorded transmittance for the BNC membrane with CDs loading capacity of 15.11 ± 2.47 mg cm−2 was <1% at <548 nm. Luminescent functionalization of CMC film with CDs was reported with the ability to convert UV light to blue light [48].

Antimicrobial activity of CD-BNC nanopapers were concentration-dependent and only BNC with CDs loading capacity of 71.74 ± 4.13 mg cm−2 represented antibacterial activity against both microorganisms (Table 1). No antimicrobial activity for pure BNC and BNC with CDs loading capacity of 27.19 ± 3.55, 51.47 ± 2.18 mg cm−2 was detected (Fig. 10 ). The solubility of CDs in agar media and the hydration of BNC instantly after attaching to the surface of agar can be the main reasons for the release of CDs into the media.

Following the attachment of CDs to the surface of bacteria, CDs induce reactive oxygen species (ROS) by transferring O2 molecule and production of free radicals [40]. Gram-positive bacteria are, in general, more sensitive to ROS than Gram-negative ones [53] due to the structural differences in the bacterial membrane. Consequently, in compliance with in vitro experiment, CD-BNC nanopaper depicted greater inhibitory activity on Gram-positive bacteria. According to Shahmohammadi Jebel and Almasi [49], Gram-positive bacteria were more sensitive than Gram-negative to BNC membrane containing 5% ZnO NPs, since ZnO NPs also generate harmful ROS as a possible mechanism for damaging the cell wall integrity.

The addition of water-soluble and photoluminescent carbon dots from probiotic bacteria may increase the antimicrobial/antioxidant and ultraviolet protective attributes of the polymeric system, revealing its capability for fabrication of antimicrobial/ultraviolet-blocking paper for food active packaging. However, nanocellulose, a hydrophilicity polymer, comprises a large number of hydroxyl groups on its surface and consequently, it could act as a suitable matrix for this function. In this study, a bio-based nanopaper with carbon dots and nanocellulose was successfully fabricated. For the first time, carbon dots were effectively synthesized through carbonization of cell-free supernatant, a value-added microbial by-product, of probiotic L. acidophilus with an average size of 2.8 nm and fluorescence appearance. The nanocellulose effectively entrapped carbon dots with carboxyl, hydroxyl, and carbonyl groups in its structure due to the hydrogen bonds interaction. In addition to antibacterial activity, carbon dots embedded in cellulose nanopaper also improved flexibility of nanopaper and illustrated a promising ultraviolet-blocking activity signifying superiority of this fluorescent glowing nanopaper for wrapping of food commodity and preparation of forgery-proof packaging. Howbeit, in vitro and in vivo toxicity tests are highly needed to conclude the overall safety of fabricated CDs for food application.

Seyedeh Alaleh Kousheh: Investigation. Mehran Moradi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis and Writing - review & editing. Hossein Tajik: Supervision and Visualization. Rahim Molaei: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis and Writing - review & editing.

All the authors thank the Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Urmia University, Iran for their financial support (grant number 623/PD/3). We also greatly appreciate Dr. Miguel Gama for supplying of K xylinus.
None.
